text: He lay and he dreamed, the Proclaimed one of Ibale,the Writer-king.
His head faced Kihanja, the King of others.
His feet faced Bujumba
He said, "All the earth weighs on me.
If it isn't an illicit fetus in a pregnant miss,
It's an errant heifer."
As the servants washed him at dawn,
Men suddenly appeared.
They had come from Black-spot in Mafumbo.
"Long life to the Fearless-one, Father of men!"
He answered , "Dawn came in peace."
One of the men said, "There is no peace.
We've come to you to search for it, Proclaimed one of Ibale.
In Mafumbo there's a serpent
That goes out to pasture like a calf.
In Kihanja kingdom there are marvels."
"Also at my home on the mountain," said Kato.
"There is an ekikomakome lizard basking in the sun.
'You sir,' it says, 'Where can I find beer?'
'For beer ask the munya lizard.
I don't know this village so well.'
The Startler of warriors asks, 'What's wrong?
Is your heart still in your chest?'
'Tell me about serpents.
Tell me about the serpents of Bugonzi River.'
'There's "Do it or don't; they'll not kill you" of Kimuli Ngundu's shrine.
There's the lujalanga serpent
My lord, and the serpent of the spirit Kashasila at Rwanda village.
There's the spitting cobras of Binengo island.
Spit the white on me–
The black brings leprosy.
Don't make my body unclean.
Inside are the lugondo serpents,
Little white ones that snatch the heart away.
There are serpents of love inside that do no harm.
And serpents that . . . '"
When they reported this to him,
[He took some butter made from . . .]
He took some butter made from new-milk
Of a heifer cow,
Two nines of mabona berries,
And eight strings of cowries.
"Go and sacrifice at Mafumbo," he said,
"Sacrifice to the ebony serpents of Black-spot in Mafumbo–
At Kitabi, Mujunwa, and Kashaka–
Serpents that cause leprosy of Nyakalanda.
They arrived there as the cows came home,
The time a guest arrives–at dusk.
They sacrificed at Black-spot in Mafumbo.
When night gave way to dawn's first light,
Out in the garden they were washing sleep from their eyes.
They saw a tree, my friend,
Its crown and branches spread way up above,
Its belly thin and straight,
And never had it shed a single splinter.
"Let's betray its secret to Proclaimed one of Ibale,
Bast that keeps enkuku beans from insect blight,
Little palm, cut from Mikoni.
From Makes-shields-laugh and Fighter of Kikabuzi and Nyamwanja."
'My lord,' we'll say, 'the tree at Mafumbo–
In Kihanja it can be adzed out for you
My lord, into a lake canoe,
My lord, of paddles nine times two.
And into another for the rushing Ngono River,
Of canoe poles nine times two.
Let them adze out a fermentation vat–
In Kihanja it can be filled with mash for you.
Let them adze out a drum, and let it roar.
In Kihanja it would reach Bujumba.'"
They came and they hit the Proclaimed one of Ibale with this news.
They said, "The tree spreads out in all directions."
He answered, "Send to the ironsmiths of Kihanja."
They hurried and sent for Bikonya.
They sent for Lwabitwaga.
To Itongo village they hurried and sent for Kalugila.
They sent for Lukonwa.
They sent for Kagote.
The carpenters–I'll not deceive you–
They sent for Kahimbile, child of Lukola.
They-have-a-reason they took from Kagabilo village.
[He took some butter made from new-milk of a heifer cow,]
"Adze out for me," he said, "a lake canoe–
On Bumbiile Island I would greet Kikuli.
Adze out for me a beer vat–
I would ferment mash for Kihanja and Bujumba.
Adze out for me a roaring drum–
I would make them hear in Lukala and Bujumba.
Go adze out for me some vessels,
A milk pot to display at the hearth
A ladle that does not split.
Adze out for me a bow, the Noble curve."
Butter from the new-milk of a heifer they laid beside the tree,
And their axes, too.
When night gave way to dawn's early light,
Of axes they saw no mubilizi herb,
Of butter they saw no mulinzi wrapper,
Of cowries they saw no raffia strings.
Laughing until it nearly split his sides,
And rushing at the tree withan axe held high,
Kashega of Itongo drove iron into wood.
Said the tree, "You man, you've stung me.
You've stung me all over.
You've not left a part.
Inside are lugondo serpents,
Others that snatch the heart away
And serpents that are white.
You've stung me.
You've spat on me all over.
But what is my blame? Why make my body tremble?
Go tell the king, the Proclaimed one:
'Let them adze you into a lake canoe'?
I won't go on my back!
'Let them adze you into a fermentation vat'?
I won't be warmed by fire!
'Let them adze you into a zither. Sing out like this one'?
I won't be pulled with strings!
'A drum–cry out for many'?
I won't be knocked about by sticks!
'Let them adze you into a fermentation vat'?
I won't be warmed by fire!
'A mash-press vat–press mash among royal followers'?
I won't be kneaded by fists!
Go tell the King, Lugomola of Ibale,
'In Kihanja you may be He who stirs what can't be sliced.
But in Mafumbo you have been stopped by Mwata.
This tree made Kyandekwe Kahinda be bad.
It entered the rivers of Bushagala Itongo.
In Kafunjo it makes Kakayange be bad.
In Ilogelo it makes Kabogoiga be bad.'"
It said, "In upper Kihanja, stop your carving."
[He said, "Consult diviners. Let us find out."]
When they told the Bast that keeps enkuku beans from insect blight,
Little palm, cut from Mikoni.
From Makes-shields-laugh and Fighter of Kikabuzi and Nyamwanja.
He said, "Consult diviners. Let us see what kills the axe-defying tree."
They divined four days.
On the fifth came their report:
"The tree will come in four years' time to topple you, Lugomola."
He said, "What shall I do, Bahamba of Lweju of Mushendwa,
Arrows that never lick the earth,
Kafumu of Mugunje,
All you children of Imalila?
What can I send to kill this tree?"
The King of [. . . ]
He said, "Shall I send to . . ."
One said, "Send word of it to Mugasha of Isheshe."
When they said this to the Proclaimed one,
The King said "Run to Wamala
Essential killer,
Bows and bends,
Supple one of Ndahula,
Papyrus stands rock up and down.
His son is Kagolo
Babbler-wobbler,
Decorated all over,
Corners of Lutungi village of Kamanzi.
They cut it in Nongo forest–it fell in Kalushekya.
Honey in the rock cannot easily be drawn.
He killed a man in Kihanja–He boasted among his affines in Kiziba.
He went and he withdrew from Sesse
The hidden serving girl of Mugasha
With his copper spear.
He said "Hurry, hurry, hurry, hurry, hurry.
Go to the Stauncher's place
Bring eight entembe seeds.
Those are the coffee berries of Mugasha
And of Mugasha's hidden maiden."
He said, "Goto Lweya and Lwazi,
Where they churn but do not rub themselves with butterfat,
They've become accustomed to the rain, and warm themselves by moonlight as by sun,
A child never opens the door for them at sunrise–
Bringing firewood she finds them as awake at dawn as at sunset.
Where a woman grinds millet with her husband hoisted on her back.
Where a dog yearns for the shaving knife
Those who traveled down inside the canoe were eight.
The helmsman was the ninth.
Rugomora said, "Go to Ihangiro to Kishashwe's place.
Go and get a virgin pot that has never cracked
Or even cooked a person's plantains.
He said, "Go to Stauncher's place.
Have him adze me a ladle that will not split,
Of Mother Nyakalembe of Kabambagwa and Nyamwema,
Iron ruled by Kabagala,
Which bent itself to cultivate,
Of Mukashuga and Galengaine in Kihanja of Malagwe and Manunga.
He said "Hurry to the copper spear."
He said "Go and take it from the place of Bwogi, the Separator
The Muzinza says it angrily at Kimbugu among the Basingo clan.
He killed a man with a big penis.
I found women's vaginas mourning,
"He's killed the Testicle-shaker."
No one ate from the kitchen pots–
A terrible thing–the pots lay empty, rocking back and forth.
In Kimbugu he came from among the Basingo.
He said, "Run and ask Sun,
Great branches of Luhuga,
Child of others.
They make no sound, of Luyenje.
Raised, imploring arms he favors,
Among the elders he speaks first.
When he had finished with all of them–
The eight loving ones–
He said "Take these matters to Mugasha,
Water dweller, Wide strider, with ntembe seeds.
Say, 'Conquor, provider,
Please give the King your axe,
Cleaver of mugege wood,
Splitter of dried mukoni trees,
So he may go and cut Muhata with it.'"
He said, "Sacrifice at Bwigula to the Catcher and the Preceeder of cold,
The Ever-satisfied one of Bwigula village.
May Lukebela be victorious.
And may he give us his canoe Kingdom-size
And the second, Compassion.
They paddled.
And when they arrived,
They brought the tribute to the spirit-medium Lukebela.
They sacrificed to Catcher.
They sacrificed to Preceeder of cold,
The Ever-satisfied one of Bwigula village.
When night gave way to the early light of dawn,
He gave them the canoe Kingdom-size
And the second, Compassion.
I heard the paddles cry out in unison
And when they had sailed out on Lake Victoria,
They sang, "If it's dead, toss it.
The rotten one has none to bury it."
They saw a bellowing cow.
'Mother,' it said, 'mother,
May what is purchased fall in the water.'
When the grasshopper was to travel,
His father sacrificed to the lake wind Ndahula."
They paddled and reached Bumbiile.
They sacrificed to Kikuli.
They sacrificed to Kisho.
They sacrificed to Nyabusana and to Nyabuyonga.
They took the lake toward Binengo.
They went to Bugoziba.
They went to Bukwaya and Kinagi
They went to Musalala and Lumondo Islands,
My lord, from there they went on foot.
They traveled and they rested at the home of Thunderstorm in Katakubwa.
They met Thunderstorm,
Drinks not beer,
Essential warrior.
A gray fowl in his fingers.
"Long life to the Fearless one, Father of men!
This greeting is not ours. It is that of [Rugomora.]"
He answered, "Dawn came in peace."
They replied, "But peace is absent.
We must go to Mugasha, Water dweller,
We'll go to Mugasha, Water dweller,
Whose touch kills,
Whose mediation pacifies,
Of planting and earthquakes,
Father of Rain, the Cloudburst,
Urine that is drunk,
Causes crops to grow in dense profusion,
Hurler of Destruction,
Unraveler of sheep herds,
Banana plants that cultivate themselves,
King with the little feet of a kafunzi bird."
He said "Now that you've come to Kyashalashala,
Paddle night and day: from Kyashalashala
Pour yourselves on to Sesse of Katanda, Mugasha's home.
You will meet Drinks not beer.
My lord, Drinks not sweet banana pulp."
We paddled night and day.
weye weye
They paddled night and day.
From Kyashalashala they poured themselves on to Sesse of Katanda,
The Catcher and the Hardened one.
They found Drinks not beer, Drinks not sweet banana pulp.
He was doing a winnowing dance on the beach at Kilembeka,.
They kneel and salute.
"Long life to the Fearless one, Father of men!
This greeting is not mine. It is that of King Rugomora.
Long life to the Fearless one, Father of men!
This greeting is not mine. It is that of the Writer.
My lord, Rugomora's royal stool
He took from the home of Wamala in Bitoma,
Supple-one of Ndahula.
The reedy swamp rocks up and down.
Kagolo, the Babbler-wobbler, is his son,
Decorated all over,
Corners of Lutungi village of Kamanzi,
From Sesse he took Mugasha's hidden serving girl."
Mugasha's laughter almost split his sides.
The pot that never for a person cooked–they brought it out for Mother Nyakalembe
of Kabambagwa and Nyamwema
The white knife that comes from Mwizi's in Itongo.
His laughter almost split his sides.
My lord, provider,
Entembe seeds are Mugasha's coffee berries.
His laughter almost split his sides.
While men were making their report,
She emerged, wife Nyakalembe,
Of Kabambagwa and Nyamwema,
Iron ruled by Kabagala.
"Where do all these men come from?" she asked.
"They're traveling about," he said.
"They come from Kyamutwara,
Messengers of Rugomora.
They came to borrow Kicks-over-trees, my axe.
I hear they have a tree they call Muhata.
Give these men gifts.
Let's not bring shame to ourselves or to our Sesse Island."
He took a Lake Victoria fish trap
And caught some little fish.
We call them otuyamba in Kihanja.
–Now they're the ones that make the plantains flee–
They were the coffee berries he offered them.
They ate four days but did not finish the basket.
He cut the plantain falls-to-earth-still-green–the kyolele type.
They ate four days but did not finish the stalk.
The cassava was ekikulu medicine root;
They never finished it.
He called Butterfly,
Man without strengh,
Mugasha's favorite at Sesse.
He said "Call my man Leg-over.
Let him go outside and beat my drum They-die-but-they've-been-told.
He went outside and called him.
He said, "Leg-over,
Take out Mugasha's drum and beat They-die-but-they've-been-told."
Beaten with a single drumstick, it roared out from the drumming place.
It went to Sina,
It went to Ibanda,
It went to Bunyonya villages,
It went to Bugonzi Babukooya Island,
To Musalala and Lumonda Islands.
iyoo
To Kinagiha Ilamba village,
To Bumbiile Island of Mukwi,
And Kiyagazi village on Nyabulo Island.
What was borne on water
Travelled along, then stood up tall at Mazinga.
What was borne by way of our own Kyamutwara
Passed through Karagwe
And called together people even in Rwanda.
What was borne in the direction to his left–
Bugabo of Kateite and Buganda–
Travelled along, then stood up tall in Bunyoro.
The nature-spirit that attended first was Overcast.
The nature-spirit that attended Sesse first was Overcast.
Mugasha said, "Sit a bit. I haven't yet inquired what I need to know."
While we were there,
Rain attended,
The Cloudburst,
Urine that is drunk,
Causes crops to grow in dense profusion,
Hurler of destruction,
Legs hold rain and ripe bananas,
Hips accompany sweet banana mash.
My friend, of the potters whose skill is not to shape a pot and fire itat once.
He said "Sit a bit. I havn't sent anyone yet."
Sun attended,
Great branches of Luhuga.
Child of others.
Ariser with the dawn.
Among elders he speaks first.
He said, "Sit a bit. I haven't sent a spirit yet."
While we were there,
Night attended,
Distant sound of women's wailing song,
Bringer of lions and leopards,
Darkness is his companion.
He said, "Sit a bit. I haven't sent anyone yet."
Nsenene attended, Big chest
[praise of nsenene not understood]
He said, "Sit a bit. I haven't sent anyone yet."
While he was there in the forecourt [Interruption]
Thunderstorm attended, Essential warrior.
He attended, a gray fowl held between his fingers.
Thunderstorm attended, Essential warrior.
Thunderstorm attended, Essential warrior.
A gray fowl held between his fingers.
He struck between the gateposts of the forecourt.
Mugasha arose from his copper stool.
He said, "Thunderstorm, you're drunk!
If you kill the Proclaimed one's men,
Don't swing your feet around and report to me.
If you kill the Proclaimed one's cattle,
Don't you dare report to me."
Thunderstorm answered, "My lord, I go for the Mafumbo tree.
There's no ill will that takes me."
I heard him call them loyal ones.
"Chant your praise," he said. "Go off to war.
Overcast, you are the spirit who attended first."
"I go to lie on the Mafumbo plot
The nights I stay there will be eight.
I'll cut what-cannot-be-turned-to-firewood by man."
"Be off," he responded.
"For children it is a time of fevers.
Rain attended, the Cloudburst.
He said, "My lord, I'll go and lie eight nights and eight days."
He said, "I'll cut what-cannot-be-turned-to-firewood by man."
"Be off," he responded.
"In Kihanja, papyrus islands sweep down a swollen Ngono river."
Sun chanted his praise.
He said, "The days I'll fall there will be eight,
And eight nights will I arise.
I'll cut the tree that-cannot-be-turned-to-firewood by man."
"Be off," he responded.
"Kihanja and Bujumba have a great, hot, drying sun."
shshshsh
Night chanted his praise,
Distant sound of women's wailing song, Time for reclining.
He said, "My lord, The days I'll lie there will be eight."
He said, "And eight nights will I arise.
I'll cut what-cannot-be-turned-to-firewood by man."
"Be off," he responded.
"Rugomora sends to know the meaning of such darkness."
Nsenene chanted his praise, Big chest [praise not understood].
He said, "The days I go to cut the tree are eight
And eight nights will I spend.
The daytimes I fall from the sky will be eight.
I'll cut the tree that-cannot-be-turned-to-firewood by man."
"Be off," he said.
"In Kihanja they go out to gather with bushel baskets."
Thunderstorm chanted his praise, Essential-warrior.
"My lord, the days will be covered over.
I'll close the sky with a single cloud
That begins at Sesse of Katanda
And stretches out to Karagwe of Nono.
Drizzling rain I'll add to the overcast.
They'll take the infants from the courtyard.
Whatever's spread to dry, they'll bring in, too.
I'll cut the tree that-cannot-be-turned-to-firewood by living being."
"Be off," heanswered.
"Return with your reports."
His laughter almost split his sides.
A canoe he loaded made of a raffia palm.
A provision he packed for them of ekikulu root, dog-washer.
They said the provision he packed for them was cassava.
The plantain he cut for them was falls-to-earth-still-green–the kyolele type.
Sawdust,
My lord, he packed for them: it was their flour.
He tossed them in the boat.
At Kilembeka shore Mugasha pushed.
No nature spirit paddled.
He just stood at Sesse and pushed
And away it rocked across Lake Victoria.
The current that took them from Sesse of Katanda,
My lord, My lord, in Kihanja it pushed them down to Bwigula.
Sacrifices had already been made there
To the Catcher
And the Precursor of cold
And the Ever-satisfied one of Bwigula village.
What joy there was!
That day they climbed the mountain pass to Kilinzi.
In Fragrant-trees, my kings.
In Boundaries and Mayondwe,
They saw the tree in the direction of Buzinza.
It left Kihanja in Mafumbo
And appeared in the direction of Buzinza.
They trod the road to Angrydog
–They were on the Kazindo road–
At Ntungamo they came to Mawela.
They climbed to Ishenge
They came to Katoke,
Kimbugu, and Nyamugunda.
At Ihangiro they crossed the gully at Muyenje.
Thunderstorm stood at Ukindi.
He went and showed himself at Lwazi.
He cut off the tree's route to Buzinja.
It came to Mafumbo of Kawe Kitabi and Mujunwa.
As you and I–they stood that close–
He sent . . .
Thunderstorm sent Overcast.
He said, "Begin. Cut down the tree, and let's go home.
You are the spirit who attended first at Sesse."
The days overcast hunched over the sky were eight,
And the nights he arose from were eight.
For children in Kihanja it was a time of fevers.
But it seemed forbidden that the tree would shed a single leaf.
The days Rain fell were eight,
And the nights he arose from were eight.
In Kihanja, my kings,
Papyrus islands swept down a swollen Ngono river.
But the tree seemed forbidden to shed a single leaf.
The days Sun's heat fell were eight,
And the nights it arose from were eight.
But it seemed forbidden that the tree would shed a single leaf.
The days the Ensenene fell were eight,
And the nights they arose from were eight.
Royal women had no nkanga baskets at all, at all.
My lord, in upper Kihanja they had to gather them in . . .
They gathered them in milk pots.
But it seemed forbidden that the tree would shed a single
iyayaya
Essential-warrior chanted his praise.
He closed in a cloud that began at Sesse of Katanda
And streched to Karagwe of Nono.
To Karagwe of Nono it stretched.
Drizzling rain fell:
Whatever had been put to dry they brought inside.
They took their infants in from the courtyard.
Then lightening flashed, struck the tree, and broke off single branch.
The tree called out again, "Thunderstorm, Essential-warrior,
My lord, you've stung me. You've stung me all over.
You've not left a part.
Inside are lugondo serpents,
Others that snatch the heart away,
Little serpents of love.
In there are the serpents of Kamuyaya.
Spit the white on me.
The black brings leprosy.
You common flunky, don't pollute my body.
Go, speak to Mugasha, the Water dweller of Sesse.
Say, 'You may be He who stirs what can't be sliced.
But at Buzongola you've been thwarted by Muhata.
The tree defeated Rugomora,
And now it has defeated you.
It will not be adzed by a living being.'"
Anger seized Thunderstorm.
He didn't wait for the other nature spirits.
Borne aloft by a cloud,
He dropped down at Lyakatana,
Bawling tears in four, eight furrows on his face,
A sweet potato mound the ninth.
He hit him with the news, the Water dweller.
Spraddle-legged, of ntembe seeds,
Planting, and earthquake,
Father of Rain, the Cloudburst,
Urine that is drunk,
Causes crops to grow in dense profusion,
Child of love.
Nyantende bore him,
Nyambubi molded him to shape.
He ended the barrenness of his father and mother.
Of the skilled potters, my lord, not those who shape and fire at once.
He reported, "Lord, it says you may be He who stirs what can't be cut.
But at Buzongola you've been thwarted by Muhata.
The tree defeated Rugomora,
And now it has defeated you."
"Was it to me the tree proclaimed these words
Or was it to Rugomora?" Mugasha asked.
"My lord, it was to you the tree proclaimed these words."
He called Butterfly, Mugasha's favorite.
Man without strength.
He said, "Call Leg-over to me.
Have him go outside and beat my drum,
I go to war myself, They-die-but-they've-been-told..
Butterfly went out,
He called Leg-over.
He struck it with one stick, Leg-over.
The roar went to Sina.
It went to Ilamba.
It went to Binengo.
It went to Bugoziba.
It went to Bukwaya.
It went to Latakubwa Bashome and Lumondo.
Kinagi and Ilamba.
The roar that was borne by the lake toward Buganda
Rose up in Bunyoro.
The roar that went straightaway to our Kihanja
Through Karagwe it stepped to Rwanda.
The roar that was taken in the direction of the lake
Travelled along and rose up in Buzinja at the Mazinga islands.
One who attended that day . . .
As Mugasha stood there in the forecourt
He was surprised by Tortise,
Can drums' roar harm me?
There were two,
And they never made it past the vestibule.
One became sandals
The other, a helmet for Mugasha.
Hippopotomi attended,
Of the snuff infusion, One roar kills, [rest of praise not understood]
Mugasha said, "If you would go with me to war.
Lie on your backs here on my land.
Let them flay you, let them take your hides to wear.
They agreed, and they lay on their backs.
They flayed them, and they set their hides to dry
Out in the little sun –- not over hot, like water coming to a boil.
They scraped the dried hides.
They sewed them.
He took the two hippopotomi,
The first he tied over his shoulder as a cloak,
The second he took for his leather kilt.
Rainbow attended, the Pouncer.
When he came into the forecourt
He did not make it past the vestibule.
Mugasha said, "Tall-fire, you come from my own belly.
Now I'll tie you for a belt around my waist.
I won't leave you behind when I go to war.
Crocodile attended.
When Mugasha saw it, he said, "You Ngogo River croc,"
He said, "Come here. Let's go to war together.
In my hands, you'll be a dagger in its sheath.
Moon attended.
–You know, when she's above, she shows the way to all the retinue.
And the American tried a raid on her but failed.
Perhaps Mugasha can bring her down.
shushushu
He said, "Come, show your beauty.
I'll put handles on you there and there,"
He said, "You'll go under my arm. You'll be a shoulder bag.
You are the afterbirth in which I came, my kings.
Under my arm you'll go, a shoulder bag.
While he was there, the Cloudburst,
While he was there, the Germinator of crops
The enshungu wind he took from Bugabo
The enkomaizi wind from Buzinja.
The omuyanja wind of Machunda's home. . .
[Praise of Machunda, king of Ukerewe, not understood]
Rain said, "I attend. I've come to go to war."
He said,"I'll go to war with you.
I agree on what's to be hollowed out."
While he stood there in the forecourt,
Out came Nyakalembe.
He said, "My wife, are you still here?
Hurry to Katakubwa village.
Go and gather 'rock hair' plants for me.
Go with my serving girls.
Wrap it for me. It's my tobacco.
Wrap packets nine-times-two for me."
Off she hurried, Nyakalembe,
Of Kabambagwa and Nyamwema,
Iron ruled by Kabagala.
With serving maidens she went to Katakubwa village among the hills.
She gathered "rock hair" there.
She wrapped tobacco packets nine-times-two.
When Nyakalembe finished bringing them,
I saw: She cut a plantain for the guests: a falls-to-earth-still-green –- the kyolele type.
I saw: She wrapped eikulu root for cassava
I saw: Tiny bukanda fish she caught, eight fish traps worth.
shushushu
She was confounded Nyakulembe
Of Kabambagwa and Nyamwena.
She said, "My husband has become the Fighter . . ."
She said, "When he wants to go to war,
I see he packs everything into the moon!"
She said,"My husband!"
She said, "Let me chant my praise and go to war."
"Don't go!"
I heard Nyakalembe: "You are . . .
He's gone before us in the wilderness–what do we fear?
In the wilderness, what do we fear?
In the wilderness, what do we fear?
In the wilderness, what do we fear?
I cooked.I preserved. I returned. I went.
Why wrap it up in cloth?
Because it will be chafed and stinging.
shushushu
Why wrap it up in cloth?
Because it will be chafed, be chafed, and stinging.
White one, you are joyful.
White one,you are joyful.
She is joyful in the reign.
She is joyful in the reign..
She is joyful in the reign.
She is joyful in the reign.
He set out,the Cloudburst.
He set out, the Urine that is drunk.
He set out, the Germinator of crops.
The canoe, he tossed into the Lake.
He pushed – no nature spirit paddled.
The current that took them from [Sesse] of Katanda
In Kanyangeleko it landed them . . .
In Kanyangeleko it landed them at Bukala.
He found them there
He stopped to mediate a case, Mugasha.
He found Kishaka and Lwazi villages
Had taken up their shields to finish one another off.
The way he planted his leg at Lwazi,
Lwazi retreated to upper Kanyangeleko Kingdom.
And Kishaka retreated to the water.
Those Kihanja mountains won't see each other again to fight.
When he came to the Igogo river,
He found Mount Bugwantula,
My lord, and the Bean-spiller
Had taken up their shields to fight.
Mugasha stopped to mediate the mountains' case.
The way he planted his one leg,
Water came from Kabale –
Kyetema stream and Big-waters of the Ngogo.
He said, "Those Kihanja mountains won't see each other again to fight."
shushushu
In our section, he made a watering draw at the ford on the Ngogo.
He made a watering draw at Trench-sides ford and Carry-under-the-arm.
He made a watering draw at Leopard's place.
The place his wife passed,
The ford of Kilalama Kashekulo,
Is where Nyakalembe found her grinding stones.
My lord, when he arrived,
He made a watering draw at Lushala ford.
He found that Mubembe mountain
And the other one were fighting
He climbed the elder Mubembe . . .
He found the mountains of Kinoni and Mubembe
Had taken up their shields to fight.
He stopped to mediate the mountains' case, Mugasha.
The way he planted his one leg:
– Water's-roar is the ford of Kashokelo –
Those mountains won't see each other again to fight.
My lord, he cut the mountain of Bugabwa.
He cut the ford of Chalky-place.
He came to [the spirit medium] Lukebela's home in Bwigula.
He found he'd already sacrificed to the Catcher.
He found he'd already sacrificed to Precursor-of-the-cold,
And the Ever-satisfied one of Bwigula village.
What gladness took him from there!
He cut the mountain pass at Kilinzi village.
He stood at Fragrant-trees.
He climbed and came to Mayondwe.
Then he was greatly pleased. He sat, Mugasha.
He spoke to his wife, Nyakalembe.
He said, "Give me the tobacco rock-hair we wrapped in packets nine-times-two."
He said, "Since we left the lake I haven't had a smoke."
shushushu
Mugasha opened all the packets.
All the rock-hair made one pipefull.
He stood at Fragrant-trees.
Fire he ordered from Bwigula.
When he turned the bowl of his pipe,
From Kyegomola he reached its stem to Kagondo.
When he blew out smoke,
A mist enclosed Kihanja Kingdom.
My lord, when he closed his eyes in pleasure,
We saw the darkness fall.
He laughed a laugh and showed his teeth, provider–
My lord, the moon shone down on Bwigula.
He said, "But I've come, of course, to battle with Muhata."
In the place he sat to rest – Kaboya – he placed a stream.
He cut Busheleka stream.
He cut Bwanja.
The road brought him up to the seasonal swamp at Shabani's place–
Where his serving girls danced singing in a circle
Formed by Kyenshushe and Kashozi mountains near Maziba and Mayondwe villages.
shushushu
He made the watering place of Nyamilembe and Biina.
He cut Pouring-water and Lugege.
He cut Tying-up and Little-packages.
"I'll go to Itoju village and report."
And at the shrine of Mugasha
He cut Kyamugasha (Water's flow).
He cut Dog-eater.
He cut Old Lungfish.
He cut Kayoya.
He hurried and made the waters at Where-did-it-come-from.
My lord, it is the pond at Itoju village.
He descended to Little-streams.
He made the watering draw at Ntabo.
The place he truly played at
Was the seasonal swamp of Kabakimbo–
Where his wife, Nyakalembe awaited him–
And the pond of Pasturage at the home of He-who-is-not-roared-at.
At Katoke he cut the seasonal swamp of Heart's-end.
He made the watering draw at I-agree.
In Ihangiro he made the watering draw in the gully at Muyenje.
He sighted the tree as it fled toward Buzinja.
He set out on the lake at Rwazi.
He started toward Shoshwa Island and Mazinga
When he had been keeping it before him,
When he'd come to border of Ihagiro Kingdom,
He made a draw on the river of May-they-tell-the-tale, my kings.
It is the very Boundary-keeper of Buzinja and Ihangiro.
shushushu
He cut the waters of Self-seconder in Itongo,
And from Self-seconder he came to Muleba.
When he reached Muleba,
In the seasonal marshes there,
He sighted the Mafumbo tree.
– As you and I are: Mugasha was that close –
He released the Butterfly,
"Go lie among the branches
And say this, 'Cook my porridge fast. It won't have time to cool.
This day he'll lie at Lulama.
Look behind you – here comes Mugasha.
His butterfly already flits among your branches.
Mugasha has not come to play with the Muhata tree.'"
When he saw the little one clutch the tree
He released the enshungu wind of Bugabo.
The omuyanja wind of Machunda's home.
The omwilombe wind from Sesse
The nkomaizi wind from [Buzinja].
They went "Ya-a-a"
And shook the tree until it reeled as though drunk.
When he had the Adze-conqueror reeling drunk,
He planted his foot to uproot the tree,
He planted it there at Copper village.
The water that springs from that place is Rushing-sound,
Is Pouring, is Kyabakoba Seen-by-few,
Is Kalebe and Kyanyabasa.
He cut Kansinda stream, the water source at Nkenge.
In Kafunjo village at Thin-man's home in Kiziba
The waters met to come to Shangano.
They flowed to the west.
He planted his foot in the direction of Buzinja of Nshanshame.
The water that sprung forth from there forms Buligi pond.
The river that swings from side to side from there is the Muleleizi,
Which descends and becomes the Earth-mover and Blood River,
And which descends to Byakalemela, my kings.
The way he planted his single foot . . .
Extending one arm out into Muleba's marshes,
Holding the tree by its branches,
He lifted it up and out completely, to its very roots.
Its branches he showed to the sun
Its roots he showed to the sun.
Then he thrust the branches downward.
He turned them towards the earth.
He said, "I'll cut down what could not be chopped by living being."
When he went to look at how to cut the tree,
He rinsed it with a pouring rain.
"I'll wash the roots," he said. "They'll never grow again."
He placed a river where he plucked it out.
"I'll go up to Kihanja and report.
I'll go and report to Rugomora."
He cut between Kitabi village and Mujunwa
And Kashaka and the Leprous snakes of Nyakalando.
At Onion village the place he sat to rest
Became Bulembo stream on the mountain.
– That mountain made him tired, Mugasha –
He made his ascent to Nyawaibaga.
He shuffled through the valley that starts from there and goes to Itongo.
The place he sat down to rest
Became Kahunguhungu spring in Itongo.
He cut the rocky outcrops at Lutega's home.
He hurried along and cut the waters of Bugoma.
He cut the waters of Nyabwetangale.
He cut the marsh of Nyabwilulilo.
He cut the waters of Nyamakonjwa.
– Kamachumu had not yet become a town –
He made the spring of Kamilabala flow.
My lord, he cut Lwachwilebitaba stream.
At our place – let me not mislead you –
He cut the waters of Sandy-stream.
He cut thewaters of Short-arm, provider.
At Itunzi village he cut Canoe-smasher in Bugenda village.
My lord, he cut the marsh of . . .
He made the watering draw of Kishalu
Where his wife got white clay,
The Beautifier of milk pots.
"Let me report to Rugomora."
He cut Short-arm stream.
He hurried along and cut Katunga marsh.
He left his footprints there to tell the story.
At King Rugomora's forecourt in our own Ibale, he knocked together the royal gateposts.
He uprooted all the plantain plants in Kagabilo
And from there he strolled without a care to our home.
The forecourt is the Bugonzi river.
The place he sat to rest in the royal forecourt of Kagembo.
He set his bow and quiver down
And dug a rocky outcrop, my kings,
The Hinda's rocky outcrop in the royal forecourt.
The place that he descended now is the Columns of Mugasha,
Where his wife Nyakalembe observed,
"We came here once in the rains,
But now the dry season has turned to drought."
So he struck his one foot,
And water sprung out there at Kagasha village:
Muleleizi stream flowed down to the Elephant-swallower river.
He was brought by the low road at Kwesigabo's place.
He cut Mugalika stream.
The River Kamaiile bubbled up.
He cut the water at Komwangushu's home.
At the home of Mister-Big at Lutete.
He made the water of Kashangula flow.
He hurried along and cut the water of Kanamile.
The water that flows to the west
Arrives at Kimaka watering draw in Nyakigando.
That day – Let me not lead you astray –
The water ran down – Let me not lead you astray –
Ran down to Mikoni stream.
He hurried along to Tabooed-hoe village where water flows from a cleft.
He crossed over and came to Rwija.
He found Strength-eater tree, which had long stood there,
The mutoma tree of Rwija.
He aimed his foot at it. It fell.
In Kyamutwara Kingdom they respect not what they see.
They said Strength-eater did not fall, my kings.
It merely lay down on its back to rest.
He made Rwija rocky outcrop a watering place.
He descended to the Nyakijuganwa fields in Kafunjo
Where he stayed four days.
. . at Ikimba lake.
The place his wife placed her feet
Became Kashwa and Kajunje.
He did not lie there, the Cloudburst.
He took the road to Bwelanyange village.
Sesse, Kailongo, Luhunga villages,
Mugajwale and Kakalonto villages.
He walked to Kabwenge
And was taken by the road to Lukoma.
When he reached Karagwe's plantain groves
He cut the grassy waterway at Bujuluga.
At Bwelanyange at the Muhinda's home, he knocked gateposts together.
"I've come to report," he said.
He finished his business and did not sleep there.
He set out toward Rwanda.
The leg that faced the west
Became the water that flows to Bugege.
And the water that descends into Kigali
That goes eastward toward the Lake
Is the Kagera river, the high road to Kyambagaza,
My lord, the way that brought him
Straightways to Nkenge.
He descended at the home of Thin-man.
There he is at Lukunyu landing
Where the Kagera steps into Lake Victoria.
The river it was that brought him to the Sesse Islands.
The floating plants you see in the Kagera that do not travel
Are like serving maidens who welcome a traveler from Rwanda.
Muzee's wife: You've worked hard.
Mzee: I did, but I refused to say where it came from.
